speaker: interviewer
text: You got to the United States, and who-- who financed your trip?

speaker: interviewee
text: The trip to the United States was financed in two parts. The part getting us out of France and into Spain was financed by the American Society of Friends, who operated in Switzerland. And the American Society of Friends is better known to the public as the Quakers, the Mormon church. They unequivocally paid for that portion of the trip.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you said--

speaker: interviewee
text: Then, from Madrid to the United States, the trip was paid by the American Joint Distribution Committee. Although the trans-Atlantic, the ship's passage, they asked to be reimbursed. And my uncle and I did that.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Tell me about how you found your parents, or what happened there.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. I did not find my parents. My brother found my parents. My brother was a paratrooper in the army and stationed in the Pacific. And in 1945 after the Armistice was signed, he hitched a ride on an airplane to Tokyo and from there, on another US military plane to Shanghai.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he simply asked a rickshaw driver to take him to the ghetto of the Jews. And he was in full American uniform. And the rickshaw person took him to this ghetto. And he stopped a person on the street, and he said to this person, in German, I'm looking for the Sinason family. Can you tell me where they are? Where they live?

speaker: interviewee
text: And these Jews were so intimidated by uniforms and authorities, and they had never seen an American uniform. And he was there, my brother was very tall, six feet and some, in his paratrooper boots with his white scarf and all that very much military looking. And this person says, no, I don't even know who they are. So my brother said thank you, and he went on. And this person, in the meantime, ran back to where my parents live because all the Jews knew each other, and said, there is some kind of policeman looking for you. Hide. I don't know who it is. I've never seen a uniform like this. I have no idea what this is.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my brother stopped another person in the street and said, can you tell me where the Sinason family lives? And this person said, no, I don't know. And again, this person waited till my brother was out of sight and ran to warn my parents. My brother stopped a third person, which was a young man, and he asked him the same question in German, said, you know where the Sinason family lives? And this young man said, no. And my brother looked at him, and he said, but I know you. And this man said, no, no, no, you don't know me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, yes, I know you. Your name is so-and-so, and you are my cousin. And this man just didn't know what to say, and so my brother identified himself. He says, I'm looking for my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, of course, my cousin, who knew very well where my parents lived, took him there. And this is how we found my parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they lived in this ghetto, and they worked in this munitions factory for a while. And then my mother had started a little business and did some sewing for the Japanese. And they eeked out a living, barely.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there anything that you took with you from Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: That I took with me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Anything that you happened to have?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. When I came to the United States, I had nothing. I only had the clothes on my back. And as I said, I had a little backpack, and it had a mess kit and a change of underwear. I had-- I had no papers. I had no pictures. I had--

speaker: interviewer
text: What about anything--

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: --that you have today?

speaker: interviewee
text: That I have today? Yes, I have things today that belonged to that period. My parents, although they had to leave Berlin in a great hurry, managed to pack a few things in a few suitcases. And I don't know if you can see the menorah that's behind me on the shelf. That menorah was a wedding present to my parents from my grandmother. And for some reason, my parents took that along to Shanghai. It broke in all the travels. And they brought it back to the United States. And that's how I have it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, what about-- what happened-- you said you had a lot of aunts and uncles. What happened to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was one of 10 children. He had six brothers and three sisters, and they all had two, three, and four children. About half of that family died in the Holocaust in the camps. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] One other brother and one sister of my father's survived in Shanghai. After my parents left for Shanghai, some of the other family relatives took the same option. But some wouldn't go. They were offered it, but they thought it was too dangerous.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, when did you marry?

speaker: interviewee
text: I married after I graduated college here in the United States in 1949.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who did you marry?

speaker: interviewee
text: I married an American girl that I had met while I was on vacation.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: And her name was June Meyer. She was born in Chicago.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about children?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have two children. I have a son, David, who is today a professor of accounting and finance at the University of Northern Illinois. And he has given me three grandsons.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my grandsons are Matthew, Joshua, and Adam Sinason.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you have other children?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I have a daughter who lives nearby here in Mission Viejo, California. Her name is Carol Sinason. She is not married. She is a single girl, and therefore, still has the same last name as I do.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you-- what date exactly did you come to the United States from Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came in the middle of October, 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you-- you went to New York. When did you leave New York?

speaker: interviewee
text: I left New York just three years ago. I lived all my life in greater New York. Always worked in New York City and Manhattan.

speaker: interviewer
text: What have you done for work?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have a degree in engineering and started out as a young engineer. Because of my language capabilities, I was sent overseas because at that time period, in the '50s, the United States was implementing the Marshall Plan. And we were rebuilding factories in Germany and all the other European countries.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what other things have you done?

speaker: interviewee
text: And from that I gravitated into actual project management where I was in charge of the entire project. And I like the financial end of project management better than the technical end. So I went back to school and got an MBA and eventually wound up in the banking business. And when I retired in 1986 I was with the Chase Manhattan Bank in New York, and I was senior vice president of administration. Since I opted for early retirement and I was really too restless to retire, I was offered and accepted a job at the United Nations as-- my official title was counselor. I did something called risk analysis, logistical risk analysis, which really meant how do you get things and people from one place to another in this world.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about hobbies? Tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: My hobbies? Oh, that's very simple. I have, fortunately, inherited my parents' taste for music. I'm an amateur musician. I play in an orchestra. I spend my summer vacations touring with an orchestra in Europe playing, representing the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you play?

speaker: interviewee
text: I play an instrument called a bass clarinet. And this group that I play with is called the All-American Winds Concert Band, and we represent the United States at some of the international music festivals in Europe like-- I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What about-- I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I live here in Laguna Hills. It's a retirement community called Leisure World. It's a very large community. There are about 18,000 people living here, and we have our own orchestra, a symphony orchestra, and I play with them. And my other hobbies are linguistics. I'm very much interested in the study of languages and their origins and so on and so forth. I always study some language or other.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you-- I want to ask some closing questions. And how would you describe your religious affiliation today?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am a conservative Jew. I remember my upbringing and my teachings. I observe the holidays. I observe the Sabbath. I do not keep a kosher house. I'm simply not talented in the kitchen. And just for myself, I don't bother much with food. But there being no conservative synagogue here in Leisure World, I had the choice between reform and modern orthodox. So I joined the modern orthodox group which leans more to the conservative than the reform does.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the reason that you decided to give your testimony today?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have been thinking about this for a long time, partially because many people think that it is important to have this record kept alive. And while I have not suffered as much as some others, those who went through the camps, I think that the entire Holocaust and the story of World War II survival by the Jewish people is a big jigsaw puzzle. And maybe my story is just one little piece of it. The other reason being that for a long time, my children and my grandchildren, especially, have asked me to put the story of my life either in writing or on tape. And I've never had the impetus to do this. And when I was offered an opportunity to do this interview, I thought I could accomplish both objectives at the same time.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you feel about the way that you've lived your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a very difficult question to answer in a very short period of time. I've always tried to do the right thing. I have never purposely hurt anyone or have done anything unethical, either in my business dealings or my personal dealings. I've had my ups and downs like everyone else, but I have no criminal record. I have nothing to reproach myself. I can look myself in the face in the morning when I look in the mirror. And I think that about sums it up.

speaker: interviewer
text: How has your experienced in Europe affected you-- yourself or maybe the way you raised your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Myself, I think it had a very profound effect. First of all, the fact that I have a European background gives me a different outlook on life. I value certain things in life more than others. My children have never been very much interested in the details of my story. My grandchildren have, and I think that's because nowadays the Holocaust is taught in school. It is taught to them, to my grandchildren, in school. In my children's day-- when they went to school in the '60s-- it was not part of the curriculum. So I think my grandchildren know more about the Holocaust than my children actually do. I think my European background has given me different interests and certainly has helped me career-wise.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think about the Holocaust much, or do you ever dream about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not much. I do not go around carrying it as a backpack. By that meaning that like some other people that I know who constantly talk about it, who constantly feel sorry for themselves, who bemoan the fact that if only it could have been, I could have been. I never think in those terms. What happened happened. I had no control over it. My parents had no control over it. There's no sense in retrospective bemoaning it. I do not forget it, not for one minute, and I will not let anyone else forget it. But I don't feel sorry for myself. And I don't think it has affected my children to any extent because I've never let it make a difference in my life. And even when I meet with my old friends whom I met in France and the OSE homes, we never talk about the bad things.

speaker: interviewee
text: Do I dream about the days in Europe? Occasionally, yes, I may have a nightmare. I sometimes dream about seeing German soldiers or [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] having a conversation in French with some people or thinking that I'm back then somewhere either in France or Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: It happens rarely, but it happens. I don't know why. And there's no particular occurrence during the day that pushes me in that direction. It just happens.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have there been any decisions made as a result of your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: What kind of decisions?

speaker: interviewer
text: Have there any life decisions that were made based on your experiences or that were really influenced?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think the most serious influence and the most serious decision, coming from that background, is the fact that I am a religious person, to the extent that I can be. It is too far for me to walk to the synagogue, therefore, I drive on the Sabbath because I feel that it's better to attend services than to sit here by myself. So I do what I can. But I am a Jew. I try to be an observant Jew. And I live by the Ten Commandments, and I think that is the biggest influence. And I'm grateful to God for every day that I've had since I left Europe and everything that's happened to me. And I give thanks every day.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's been over 50 years since your experience, since the end of the war. And your children, grandchildren, other people will be watching this tape. What message or legacy would you like to leave for those watching?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd like to dedicate this tape to my children and to my grandchildren and tell them and everyone else never to forget how lucky they are to live in this country, to have been born in this country, and to give thanks and [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] fight evil in any way that they can. Be good people. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Because without God's help, I would have never come here, and they would not be.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything additional you'd like to say before we show the photographs?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm very grateful for the opportunity of your letting me tell my story. I have never done so before, certainly not in this much detail and so completely. And I'm grateful for that. And I am also grateful that my children will have a record that will survive me. My grandchildren, maybe someday some great-grandchildren, will have an opportunity to see what I look like and what I sounded like and what happened to me, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to relive something that they only know about from history books, and to say to them it actually happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, why don't we stop right here? And then, we want to thank you very much for you sharing your testimony with us. And we're going to be showing photographs of you and your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: I thank you very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about this photograph, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my father, Harry Sinasohn.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where is he?

speaker: interviewee
text: He is at the French front.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where is he in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: He is the third person from the left, sitting cross-legged. There's a little X in front of him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what country was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in France. He was with the German Imperial Army during World War I.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was 1918.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about this photograph. Where are you, Henry?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of me, Henry Sinason, taken in 1935 in the Fourth Jewish Elementary School.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the country?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Berlin, Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where are you sitting?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I am sitting at the right row in the first seat, the second person, on the left, in that little white shirt, wearing glasses. That's me.

speaker: interviewer
text: From left to right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, this picture was taken in Berlin, Germany in 1937. It is of my mother, and my brother, and myself. From left to right, at the very left is my brother. His name in Berlin was Gunter Sinason. He later on in the United States changed it to George Stare. When he joined the army, he was afraid that the name might--

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: --get him into trouble. In the center is my mother, Selma Sinasohn. And on the far right is myself, Henry Sinason. This was taken in our apartment in Berlin in 1937. This is a picture of my father, Harry Sinasohn, that was taken in Berlin in 1938 for his passport.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Berlin, Germany, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of the OSE home in Chabannes called the Chateau de Chabannes where I lived in 1940 and 1941. There were a hundred children living in this house.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get the photographs that were taken of your family before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: The pictures of my family taken before the war came to me through my mother when, eventually, she and my father came from Shanghai to the United States in 1947. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is a photograph taken in France in 1940. It shows a group of students working in the leatherworking workshop run in cooperation by ORT and the OSE. I am Henry Sinason, the person all the way on the right, top row.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was taken in France? This was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --taken where?

speaker: interviewee
text: It says-- oh, I'm sorry. This was taken at the Chateau de Chabannes in France in 1940. This is a partial view of my travel pass, Henry Sinasohn, still using my silent first name of Heinz, issued by the American Friends Service Committee, which let me travel from Chabannes, France to Marseille, France in September of 1941. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is a photograph that appeared in a commemorative booklet issued by the OSE. It shows me, Henry Sinason.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am in the center of the picture, talking to the gentleman in the suit. The gentleman is Mr. Katzki of the American Joint Distribution Committee. The picture was taken at the collection point, the hotel we lived in in Marseille, France, in the middle of September, 1941, just before we left for Spain. This is a picture taken in Shanghai, China. It shows on the left, my brother, George Sinasohn Stare, S-T-A-R-E, and my mother, Selma Sinasohn. This was when my brother went to Shanghai to find my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was 1945. This is a picture taken in Shanghai, China in the year 1944, of my parents-- on the left, my mother, Selma Sinasohn, on the right, my father, Harry Sinasohn. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is a picture taken in 1981 in New York City when I was working for Chase Manhattan Bank. I was vice president in charge of administration, and I was hosting a reception for some of the bank's suppliers of checks. I am the second person from the left, Henry Sinason.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in 1981.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in 1988 at the Chateau de Chabannes, the former OSE home. And all the people in this picture are my former comrades who were at the OSE home in Chabannes with me in 1940.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am the fourth person in the middle row, the baldish-looking gentleman wearing glasses next to the lady with the white blouse.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'm Henry Sinason. It was taken in 1988. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is a picture of my son and his family. On the left is my son, David Sinason, with his wife, Karen Sinason. On the right are my three grandsons. The one on the left is Joshua, the middle one. The one behind him is Matthew, the oldest. And the little one in front is Adam. This picture was taken in 1990 when Adam was about two years old. Joshua was about six, and Matthew-- 10.

speaker: interviewer
text: What country?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: City?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Tallahassee, Florida. This is a picture of my daughter, Carol Sinason. It was taken in the year 1985 in Houston, Texas. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is a photograph showing four generations of my family. On the left is my mother, Selma Sinasohn. The little boy is my oldest grandson, Matthew. I am holding him, in the middle. I'm Henry Sinason. And to the right is my son David. This picture was--

speaker: interviewer
text: His last name is--

speaker: interviewee
text: --is David Sinason. This picture was taken in 1981 in Tallahassee, Florida. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is a picture of myself, Henry Sinason, taken in 1975 in Ridgewood, New Jersey. I'm holding my musical instrument, a bass clarinet, and it was taken after a concert where I performed with the local symphony orchestra.
